annotations.label: Intuitionistic logic

anchor.snippet: refers to systems of symbolic logic that differ from the systems used for classical logic by more closely mirroring the notion of constructive proof

annotations.note: Mathlib has the algebraic semantics (`HeytingAlgebra`) and a propositional proof-search tactic (`itauto`/`IProp`), but no formal definition of the deductive system itself.

annotations.label: BHK interpretation as standard explanation

anchor.snippet: The standard explanation of intuitionistic logic is the BHK interpretation

annotations.note: No formalization of the Brouwer–Heyting–Kolmogorov interpretation was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Semantics induce logics stronger than Heyting's

anchor.snippet: Yet such semantics persistently induce logics properly stronger than Heyting’s logic

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize the meta-statement comparing strengths of constructive semantics versus Heyting's logic.

annotations.label: Law of excluded middle (classical semantics)

anchor.snippet: This is referred to as the 'law of excluded middle', because it excludes the possibility of any truth value besides 'true' or 'false'

annotations.note: `Classical.em` (re-exported throughout Mathlib) states `∀ p, p ∨ ¬p`, the law of excluded middle.

annotations.label: Inhabitation by a proof (Curry–Howard)

anchor.snippet: that it is inhabited by a proof in the Curry–Howard sense

annotations.note: The Curry–Howard correspondence is the meta-foundation of Lean itself, but is not a declaration inside Mathlib.

annotations.label: Disjunction and existence properties

anchor.snippet: its restrictions produce proofs that have the disjunction and existence properties

annotations.note: No formal statement or proof of the disjunction/existence properties of intuitionistic logic was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Four color theorem verification

anchor.snippet: One example of a proof that was impossible to satisfactorily verify without formal verification is the famous proof of the four color theorem

annotations.note: The four color theorem is not formalized in Mathlib (it appears only in `docs/100.yaml` and `docs/1000.yaml` lists).

annotations.label: IPL basic connectives

anchor.snippet: In intuitionistic propositional logic (IPL) it is customary to use

annotations.note: The inductive `IProp` in `itauto` formalizes the IPL syntax (atoms, ⊤, ⊥, ∧, ∨, →) used by the tactic's decision procedure.

annotations.label: Hilbert-style calculus for intuitionistic logic

anchor.snippet: Intuitionistic logic can be defined using the following Hilbert-style calculus

annotations.note: No Hilbert-style deductive system for intuitionistic logic is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Modus ponens inference rule

anchor.snippet: In propositional logic, the inference rule is modus ponens

annotations.note: Modus ponens is the Lean function-application primitive, not a Mathlib declaration; no formal inference-rule object exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Generalization rules for predicate logic

anchor.snippet: To make this a system of first-order predicate logic , the generalization rules

annotations.note: No Hilbert-style predicate-logic generalization rules are formalized as Mathlib declarations.

annotations.label: Negation axioms NOT-1' and NOT-2'

anchor.snippet: If one wishes to include a connective

annotations.note: No Hilbert axiom schemata for intuitionistic negation are formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Alternative negation axioms without false

anchor.snippet: There are a number of alternatives available if one wishes to omit the connective

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize alternative axiomatizations of negation without ⊥.

annotations.label: Equivalence connective as abbreviation

anchor.snippet: The connective

annotations.note: While `Iff` exists in Lean core, Mathlib does not formalize the IPL syntactic abbreviation of ↔ in the Hilbert calculus sense.

annotations.label: Gentzen's sequent calculus LJ

anchor.snippet: Gerhard Gentzen discovered that a simple restriction of his system LK

annotations.note: Gentzen's LJ sequent calculus is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: LJ' multiple-conclusion variant

anchor.snippet: Other derivatives of LK are limited to intuitionistic derivations but still allow multiple conclusions in a sequent

annotations.note: Multiple-conclusion intuitionistic sequent calculi are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Intuitionistic theorems are classical theorems

anchor.snippet: Each theorem of intuitionistic logic is a theorem in classical logic, but not conversely

annotations.note: This meta-theorem about the proper inclusion IL ⊊ CL is not stated formally in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Double negation of excluded middle in minimal logic

anchor.snippet: Formally, it is a simple theorem that

annotations.note: No declaration of `¬¬(p ∨ ¬p)` proved in minimal/intuitionistic logic was found in Mathlib (grep returned no matches for `not_not_em` etc.).

annotations.label: Stable proposition

anchor.snippet: Propositions for which double-negation elimination is possible are also called stable

annotations.note: `Heyting.IsRegular a := aᶜᶜ = a` formalizes the Heyting-algebra analogue of `Prop`-level stability.

annotations.label: Stability of negated propositions

anchor.snippet: As a special case, it follows that propositions of negated form

annotations.note: `isRegular_compl` shows every complement (negation) is Heyting-regular, the algebraic analogue of stability of `¬p`.

annotations.label: Gödel–Gentzen double-negation translation

anchor.snippet: a first-order formula is provable in classical logic if and only if its Gödel–Gentzen translation is provable intuitionistically

annotations.note: The Gödel–Gentzen double-negation translation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Conjunction/disjunction relate to implication via negation

anchor.snippet: Already minimal logic easily proves the following theorems, relating conjunction resp. disjunction to the implication using negation

annotations.note: Mathlib has classical versions like `not_and_or`, `or_iff_not_and_not`, etc., but the minimal-logic intuitionistic statements are not isolated as such.

annotations.label: Non-reversibility of negation-distribution implications

anchor.snippet: However, neither of these five implications above can be reversed without immediately implying excluded middle

annotations.note: The meta-statement that reversing those implications yields excluded middle is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Existential implies negated universal

anchor.snippet: If there exists an entity

annotations.note: `not_forall_of_exists_not : (∃ x, ¬p x) → ¬ ∀ x, p x` is the intuitionistically valid direction.

annotations.label: Stronger quantifier negation theorem

anchor.snippet: And so, in fact, a stronger theorem holds

annotations.note: The strengthened intuitionistic quantifier-negation theorem cited is not isolated as a Mathlib lemma.

annotations.label: Disjointness characterizations equivalence

anchor.snippet: the special case of this equivalence with false

annotations.note: This equivalence of disjointness characterizations is not formalized as a Mathlib lemma.

annotations.label: Finite De Morgan-type variants for two propositions

anchor.snippet: There are finite variations of the quantifier formulas, with just two propositions

annotations.note: Mathlib has the classical `not_and_or`, `not_or`, etc., but not the intuitionistically restricted minimal-logic variants as isolated lemmas.

annotations.label: Constant domain principle invalidity

anchor.snippet: In predicate logic, the constant domain principle is not valid

annotations.note: The constant domain principle and its invalidity in IL are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Import-export equivalence

anchor.snippet: From the general equivalence also follows import-export , expressing incompatibility of two predicates using two different connectives

annotations.note: `and_imp : (a ∧ b → c) ↔ (a → b → c)` is the import-export equivalence, intuitionistically valid.

annotations.label: Conjunction of unrejectable propositions unrejectable

anchor.snippet: which entails that a conjunction of unrejectable propositions cannot be rejected either

annotations.note: No declaration formalizing `¬¬p ∧ ¬¬q → ¬¬(p ∧ q)` as an isolated lemma was found.

annotations.label: Excluded middle equivalent to consequentia mirabilis

anchor.snippet: Already minimal logic proves excluded middle equivalent to consequentia mirabilis

annotations.note: Consequentia mirabilis is not named or formalized in Mathlib (grep for `consequentia`/`mirabilis` returned no Mathlib hits).

annotations.label: Strengthened disjunctive syllogism

anchor.snippet: Strengthened forms still holds in intuitionistic logic

annotations.note: Strengthened intuitionistic forms of disjunctive syllogism are not isolated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Equivalence as conjunction of implications

anchor.snippet: An equivalence itself is generally defined as, and then equivalent to, a conjunction

annotations.note: `iff_iff_implies_and_implies : (a ↔ b) ↔ ((a → b) ∧ (b → a))` formalizes this equivalence.

annotations.label: Kuznetsov functionally complete connectives

anchor.snippet: As shown by Alexander V. Kuznetsov , either of the following connectives

annotations.note: Kuznetsov's functional completeness theorem for IPC is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Constable's Tarski-like completeness

anchor.snippet: a Tarski-like model theory was proved complete by Bob Constable

annotations.note: Constable's Tarski-like completeness theorem for IL is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Glivenko: no third truth value

anchor.snippet: One can prove that such statements have no third truth value, a result dating back to Glivenko in 1928

annotations.note: Glivenko's theorem is not formalized in Mathlib (grep for `Glivenko` matched only `docs/1000.yaml`).

annotations.label: Heyting algebra validity characterization

anchor.snippet: A formula is valid in intuitionistic logic if and only if it receives the value of the top element for any valuation on any Heyting algebra

annotations.note: The completeness theorem of IPC with respect to Heyting algebras is not formalized; Mathlib has `HeytingAlgebra` but no syntactic-validity bridge.

annotations.label: Open sets of the real line suffice

anchor.snippet: It can be shown that to recognize valid formulas, it is sufficient to consider a single Heyting algebra whose elements are the open subsets of the real line

annotations.note: Mathlib has the Heyting/Frame structure on `TopologicalSpace.Opens` but does not prove completeness of IPC over open sets of ℝ.

annotations.label: Validity of ¬(A ∧ ¬A)

anchor.snippet: the formula ¬( A ∧ ¬ A ) is valid, because no matter what set

annotations.note: `¬(A ∧ ¬A)` is provable intuitionistically; Mathlib has `not_and_self` and a Heyting-algebra analogue `inf_compl_eq_bot`.

annotations.label: Invalidity of excluded middle via positive reals

anchor.snippet: But the law of the excluded middle, A ∨ ¬ A , can be shown to be invalid by using a specific value of the set of positive real numbers

annotations.note: The specific Heyting-algebra countermodel for excluded middle via positive reals is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: No finite Heyting algebra suffices

anchor.snippet: No finite Heyting algebra has the second of these two properties

annotations.note: The result that no finite Heyting algebra is complete for IPC is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Kripke semantics for intuitionistic logic

anchor.snippet: Saul Kripke created another semantics for intuitionistic logic, known as Kripke semantics or relational semantics

annotations.note: Kripke semantics for IL is not formalized in Mathlib (grep for `Kripke` returned no matches).

annotations.label: Constable's weaker Tarski-like completeness

anchor.snippet: Robert Constable has shown that a weaker notion of completeness still holds for intuitionistic logic under a Tarski-like model

annotations.note: Constable's weaker Tarski-like completeness result is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Admissible rule

anchor.snippet: the situation can occur that an implication always holds metatheoretically, but not in the language

annotations.note: The concept of an admissible (but not derivable) rule is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Disjunction property of IPC

anchor.snippet: Theories over constructive logics can exhibit the disjunction property

annotations.note: The disjunction property of IPC is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Brazilian/dual-intuitionistic logic

anchor.snippet: Intuitionistic logic is related by duality to a paraconsistent logic known as Brazilian , anti-intuitionistic or dual-intuitionistic logic

annotations.note: Mathlib's `CoheytingAlgebra` is the algebraic dual relevant to dual-intuitionistic logic, but the logic itself is not formalized.

annotations.label: Minimal logic

anchor.snippet: The subsystem of intuitionistic logic with the FALSE (resp. NOT-2) axiom removed is known as minimal logic

annotations.note: Minimal logic is not formalized as a deductive system in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Intermediate logics from finite Heyting algebras

anchor.snippet: any finite Heyting algebra that is not equivalent to a Boolean algebra defines (semantically) an intermediate logic

annotations.note: Intermediate logics defined via finite Heyting algebras are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gödel–Dummett logic

anchor.snippet: Adopting this over intuitionistic logic gives the intermediate logic called Gödel–Dummett logic

annotations.note: Gödel–Dummett logic is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Classical logic axiom additions

anchor.snippet: The system of classical logic is obtained by adding any one of the following axioms

annotations.note: `peirce`, `Classical.em`, and `Classical.byContradiction` are present, but the meta-result that each individually yields classical logic atop IL is not formalized.

annotations.label: Gödel: intuitionistic logic not finite-valued

anchor.snippet: Kurt Gödel 's work involving many-valued logic showed in 1932 that intuitionistic logic is not a finite-valued logic

annotations.note: Gödel's 1932 result that IPC is not finite-valued is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gödel–McKinsey–Tarski translation into S4

anchor.snippet: it has been demonstrated that the translated formula is valid in the propositional modal logic S4 if and only if the original formula is valid in IPC

annotations.note: The Gödel–McKinsey–Tarski embedding of IPC into S4 is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Curry–Howard with simply typed lambda calculus

anchor.snippet: There is an extended Curry–Howard correspondence between IPC and simply typed lambda calculus

annotations.note: Although Lean is itself based on dependent type theory, no formal Curry–Howard correspondence between IPC and simply typed λ-calculus is stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Principle of explosion

anchor.snippet: by the principle of explosion , an existential statement implies anything

annotations.note: `absurd : a → ¬a → b` is the principle of explosion (ex falso quodlibet), intuitionistically valid.

annotations.label: Law of non-contradiction

anchor.snippet: results in the law of non-contradiction principle

annotations.note: `¬(p ∧ ¬p)` is the law of non-contradiction, intuitionistically provable; in Mathlib via `not_and_self_iff`.

annotations.label: Drinker's paradox

anchor.snippet: This inverse direction is equivalent to the drinker's paradox (DP)

annotations.note: The drinker's paradox (a classical-only existential lemma) is not formalized as a named result in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Disjunctive syllogism

anchor.snippet: A formula for which excluded middle holds can be proven stable using the disjunctive syllogism

annotations.note: `Or.resolve_left : a ∨ b → ¬a → b` is the disjunctive syllogism, intuitionistically valid.

annotations.label: Reverse law of contraposition

anchor.snippet: One notable one is the (reverse) law of contraposition

annotations.note: `not_imp_not : (¬b → ¬a) ↔ (a → b)` is the classical reverse contraposition; the forward direction is intuitionistic, the reverse needs classical logic.

annotations.label: Curry/uncurry as conjunction–implication equivalence

anchor.snippet: The equivalence formula for the negated conjunction may be understood as a special case of currying and uncurrying

annotations.note: Currying/uncurrying at the level of propositions is `and_imp : (a ∧ b → c) ↔ (a → b → c)`.

annotations.label: Negation introduction via implication of contradiction

anchor.snippet: Having proven such a mere double-negation also still aids in negating other statements through negation introduction

annotations.note: `mt : (a → b) → ¬b → ¬a` (modus tollens) implements negation introduction: from `a → b` and `¬b`, derive `¬a`.
